abstract: This article updates Freelon's (2018) concept of the 'post-API age' by tracing nearly two decades of social media data access policies across six major platforms (Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X, YouTube, Reddit, and TikTok). The authors propose a periodized history spanning API prehistory through the current era, classify present-day data access into four official regimes plus unofficial methods, and offer normative recommendations for improving platform-researcher relations. They argue that data access has become permanently contingent on platform decisions shaped by scandals, regulation, leadership changes, and commercial interests.
key_claims: Social media data access exists in a 'post-API age' where platforms can restrict or eliminate access at any time, making research conditions permanently precarious.

The history of social media data access can be divided into five periods: API prehistory (2000-2006), laissez-faire (2006-2011), authentication (2011-2018), limited options (2018-2020), and academic cooperation (2020-2023).
Current platform data access in 2025 falls into four official regimes—laissez-faire API, academic API, academic walled garden, and pay-to-play API—plus a fifth category of unofficial methods.

Platforms facing intense public scrutiny (Meta, X) tend to impose the most restrictive controls, while less controversial or newer platforms (YouTube, Reddit, TikTok) maintain comparatively open access.

Unofficial data collection methods are a necessary and legitimate component of the researcher toolkit because platforms have structural incentives to limit research that may reveal unfavorable findings.

Platform data access policies should be reformed to outsource proposal evaluation to qualified academic partners, allow downloadable data, permit dataset fixation rather than continuous deletion, avoid topic-based restrictions, charge reasonable fees, and reinstate rehydration-style sharing.
methods: Historical analysis using official platform documentation, archived web pages, terms of service, developer agreements, and the secondary research literature.
Periodization of social media API history organized around major shifts in data access regimes, extending and revising Jünger's (2021) prior history.
Comparative descriptive analysis of six platforms (Facebook, Instagram, Twitter/X, YouTube, Reddit, TikTok) across multiple dimensions of data access.
Typological classification of current data access into five regimes characterized by features such as cost, authentication requirements, and approval processes.
Normative analysis articulating recommendations across six categories: access, format, management, usage, costs, and sharing.
findings: Meta's 2018 closure of the Facebook/Instagram APIs following Cambridge Analytica marked the defining moment establishing the post-API age for social science researchers.
Twitter's 2011 shift requiring user authentication and prohibiting dataset sharing rendered tools like TwapperKeeper obsolete and raised technical barriers to entry.

CrowdTangle (2020-2024) restored some Facebook access but suffered from inconsistent historical data, strict rate limits, and inability to collect comments; its replacement, the Meta Content Library, imposes visibility thresholds (15,000-25,000 members) and a restrictive 'clean room' environment.

X's 2023 transition to pay-to-play pricing made data prohibitively expensive ($42,000-$210,000/month initially), and the company currently publishes inconsistent pricing information across its own documentation.

TikTok's research API, launched February 2023, is capped at 100,000 records per day, uses two-hour bearer tokens, limits queries to 30-day windows, and requires ongoing data refreshes that delete inaccessible content.
Reddit terminated Pushshift public access in 2023 but archived torrents remain available; Reddit's developer terms are identified as the most researcher-friendly model among the platforms studied.
EU regulations (GDPR, DSA) create geographic inequalities in data access, with VLOPs required to offer EU-based researchers access unavailable to those elsewhere.
contributions: Provides an updated, extended periodized history of social media data access covering nearly 20 years and six platforms, expanding beyond Jünger's (2021) earlier history.
Introduces a typology of four current official data access regimes plus an unofficial methods category, useful for both contemporary researchers and future historians of the field.
Offers concrete normative recommendations across six dimensions of platform data provision intended to inform policy decisions by platforms, regulators, and the research community.
Documents and critiques specific problematic provisions in current platform terms of service (e.g., continuous deletion requirements, topic restrictions, anti-data-merging clauses).
Defends unofficial scraping methods as ethically and legally legitimate components of social media research, citing supportive case law (hiQ Labs, Sandvig v. Barr, X v. Bright Data).
framing: The paper situates itself within ongoing scholarly conversations about computational social science, platform governance, and the precarity of digital research infrastructure, building explicitly on Freelon's (2018) original 'post-API age' framework and adjacent work by Bruns, Tromble, Jünger, and others. It bridges descriptive history of platform-research relations with normative platform-policy critique, engaging debates about transparency, privacy regulation (GDPR, DSA), and the political economy of data access.
